A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Daniel Dennett

1942 – 2024

The intentional stance, the narrative self, and consciousness as fame in the brain.

Daniel Dennett’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a Darwinian, anti-Cartesian, functionalist-interpretivist naturalism. That label is inelegant, but each term matters. He is Darwinian because he treats mind, content, consciousness, selfhood, and agency as products of gradual, non-miraculous evolutionary and cultural construction. He is anti-Cartesian because he rejects the picture of consciousness as an inner theater inspected by a private subject. He is functionalist because mental phenomena are explained by what systems do, not by an immaterial substance or by a simple one-to-one identification with neural states. He is interpretivist because beliefs, desires, selves, and sometimes even conscious episodes are not inner objects waiting to be discovered; they are higher-level patterns made visible from explanatory stances. And he is a naturalist because every explanatory resource must be continuous with cognitive science, neuroscience, biology, artificial intelligence, ethology, and psychology.

Dennett, who lived from 1942 to 2024, worked at the point where postwar analytic philosophy of mind, the cognitive revolution, AI, evolutionary theory, and philosophy of biology converged. His own stated project was, in effect, to understand how brains can explain minds, and his career helped move Anglophone philosophy of mind away from purely conceptual analysis and toward a coalition with cognitive science.

The central Dennettian question is not, “How can a private inner light be added to matter?” It is: How can systems with no inner witness, no soul, no intrinsic semantic glow, and no first-person epistemic magic nevertheless come to behave, speak, remember, deliberate, suffer, report, interpret, and hold one another responsible in the ways we associate with minded beings?

His answer is: through layers of design, interpretation, information processing, learned and evolved dispositions, public language, self-narration, and real but abstract patterns.

⸻

1. Historical position in philosophy of mind

Dennett enters a field structured by several major twentieth-century alternatives. Logical behaviorism tried to understand mental language through patterns of behavior and behavioral disposition. Identity theory held that mental states and processes are identical with brain states and processes. Functionalism then argued that mental states are individuated by their causal or functional roles within a system, not by their material constitution. The computational theory of mind, central to cognitive science in the 1960s and 1970s, treated mental processes as computations, often involving mental representations.

Dennett is historically close to functionalism, but not simply a standard functionalist. Standard functionalism says: a belief, desire, pain, or perception is a state with the right causal role. Dennett’s more distinctive claim is: the attribution of belief, desire, pain, or perception belongs to an explanatory stance taken toward a whole system. Mental vocabulary is not a low-level inventory of inner furniture; it is a high-level interpretive apparatus for finding predictive and explanatory order in complex agents. In this respect he differs both from reductive identity theorists, who seek type-by-type mind-brain identities, and from classical representationalists, who posit inner semantic items as the basic ontology of cognition. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s entry on mental representation explicitly contrasts representational theories with views such as Dennett’s, which deny that cognition must be explained by semantically evaluable inner mental objects in the standard realist sense.

Dennett’s first major book, Content and Consciousness (1969), already contained the architecture of his later philosophy: a theory of mental content, a theory of consciousness, an anti-Cartesian attack on the mind-body problem, and the distinction between personal-level and subpersonal-level explanation. It was based on his Oxford work under Gilbert Ryle, and later commentators have rightly seen it as the “kitchen” from which much of his mature work was prepared.

Historically, Dennett should be placed in a line that runs from Ryle and Quine through cognitive science and Darwinian naturalism. From Ryle, he inherits the suspicion that philosophers mistakenly reify mental life into inner objects, “ghostly” acts, or private theaters. From Quine, he inherits anti-foundationalism about meaning, indeterminacy of interpretation, and suspicion of supposedly given mental facts. From Darwin, he inherits the methodological imperative that mind must be explained by bottom-up processes: no skyhooks, no irreducible mental magic, no explanatory miracles. From AI and cognitive science, he inherits the idea that intelligence may be decomposed into interacting mechanisms, many of which are individually unintelligent.

His place in the field is therefore unusual. He is not a dualist, not an identity theorist, not a behaviorist, not a straightforward eliminativist, not a Fodorian realist about a language of thought, and not a Chalmers-style property dualist. He is a deflationary naturalist who tries to show that the metaphysical residues philosophers call “qualia,” “the subject,” “original intentionality,” and “the hard problem” are artifacts of bad models of mind.

⸻

2. The core anti-Cartesian commitment

Dennett’s deepest opponent is not only Descartes’s substance dualism. It is what he calls the lingering Cartesian architecture of thought: the assumption that, somewhere in the mind or brain, “it all comes together” for an inner observer.

This anti-Cartesianism has several layers.

First, there is no immaterial ego. The mind is not a nonphysical substance joined to the body.

Second, there is no inner witness even inside the physical brain. A materialist can reject the soul while still imagining a privileged neural place where experience becomes finally present to consciousness. Dennett calls this error “Cartesian materialism.” It is Cartesian not because it posits an immaterial mind, but because it preserves the structural idea of an inner theater and an audience.

Third, there is no incorrigible first-person authority. A subject’s reports are important data, but they are not revelations of private mental facts immune to scientific reinterpretation.

Fourth, there is no sharp metaphysical boundary between creatures with “real” minds and systems that merely behave as if they had minds. Intentionality, agency, and mindedness come in degrees and are often a matter of the richness, stability, and usefulness of the interpretive stance we can take toward a system.

Dennett’s own career-long strategy was sometimes summarized as “first content, then consciousness”: explain how unconscious systems can have content-like, function-like, world-directed organization; then explain consciousness as a later, more complex achievement built from such materials. This reverses the Cartesian tendency to treat consciousness as primitive and meaning as derivative from it.

⸻

3. Stances: physical, design, intentional

The most important single concept in Dennett’s philosophy of mind is the intentional stance. It belongs to a family of explanatory stances.

The physical stance predicts a system by appeal to its physical constitution and laws: mass, charge, circuitry, neural firing, chemistry, mechanics.

The design stance predicts a system by assuming that it is designed, whether by an engineer or by natural selection, to perform certain functions. We do not calculate every molecular event in a thermostat or every physical detail of a heart; we treat them as devices with functions.

The intentional stance predicts a system by treating it as a rational agent with beliefs, desires, goals, expectations, and practical sensitivity to its environment. We ask what the system wants, what it believes, and what it ought rationally to do given those beliefs and desires.

In The Intentional Stance (1987), Dennett gives his first full-scale presentation of this theory. MIT Press summarizes the view as a predictive strategy of interpretation that uses folk-psychological concepts such as belief, desire, intention, and expectation while presupposing the rationality of the entity to be understood.

This is not merely a theory about human beings. It is a general theory of how mentality becomes visible. A chess computer, an animal, a human being, a simple organism, or even a thermostat may be interpretable to different degrees from the intentional stance. The richer and more reliable the intentional interpretation, the more reason we have to treat the system as an intentional system.

This produces one of Dennett’s most controversial theses: to be a believer is not necessarily to contain a discrete inner object called a belief; it is to be a system whose behavior is successfully and deeply predictable from the intentional stance.

The intentional stance is therefore both epistemological and ontological. It tells us how we predict and explain; but Dennett also thinks that, under the right conditions, the patterns revealed by this stance are real.

⸻

4. Real patterns: Dennett’s moderate realism about belief

Dennett is often misdescribed as an instrumentalist who thinks beliefs and desires are merely useful fictions. That is too crude. His mature view is closer to pattern realism.

In “Real Patterns” (1991), he asks whether beliefs are real or whether neuroscience will show that they are fictions. He rejects the forced choice between robust inner-object realism and eliminative materialism. Beliefs are real when they are part of a pattern that supports successful prediction, explanation, and compression of information about the system.

The analogy is the center of gravity. A center of gravity is not a particle, organ, or hidden concrete thing. Yet it is not arbitrary. It is a real abstract posit within a theory because it plays a stable explanatory and predictive role. Likewise, a belief may be real without being a localized neural object. It may be real as a pattern in the behavior, dispositions, history, and environment of a whole agent.

This gives Dennett a distinctive position between eliminativism and traditional realism. Against eliminativists, he says: folk-psychological patterns are often too predictively powerful to discard. Against traditional realists, he says: do not look for beliefs as discrete inner particulars. Beliefs are more like centers of gravity, trajectories, or software-level patterns than like marbles in the head.

The resulting ontology is mildly realist, abstract, pragmatic, and anti-reductionist without being anti-physicalist. Everything is physically realized, but not every real pattern is visible at the physical level. A stock-market trend, a chess strategy, a center of gravity, a software bug, and a belief can be real even though none is a simple material object.

⸻

5. Personal and subpersonal explanation

Dennett’s distinction between personal-level and subpersonal-level explanation is one of his lasting contributions to the philosophy of psychology.

At the personal level, we explain the whole agent: the person sees, believes, desires, fears, decides, reasons, lies, regrets, imagines, or acts. At the subpersonal level, we explain the mechanisms that make these capacities possible: neural circuits, perceptual modules, memory systems, computational routines, motor programs, and information-processing pathways.

The distinction is crucial because many philosophical confusions arise from mixing levels. Brains do not literally believe propositions, regret mistakes, or see tigers in the way persons do. But persons do these things because their subpersonal systems process information in specific ways. Conversely, one cannot fully explain the person merely by listing neural events; the pattern that matters at the personal level may be lost if one changes the explanatory grain.

Dennett’s position is not that the personal level is metaphysically autonomous in a dualist sense. Rather, personal-level explanation is a real explanatory level because the whole organism displays patterns that are not perspicuous at the level of molecules, cells, or isolated neural firings.

This is central to his view of psychology. Psychology is not reducible to neurophysiology in the crude sense that every psychological generalization can be replaced by a neural one. But psychology must also avoid positing ghostly inner items. Its task is to connect intentional, personal-level patterns with subpersonal mechanisms.

⸻

6. Homuncular functionalism: explaining intelligence by decomposition

Dennett’s method for explaining mental capacities is sometimes called homuncular functionalism. The idea is initially paradoxical: explain the mind by decomposing it into smaller “homunculi,” or sub-agents. But the regress is harmless if each lower-level homunculus is less intelligent, less mysterious, and more mechanically describable than the system it helps explain.

Suppose we explain vision by saying that a visual system detects edges, tracks motion, groups objects, identifies affordances, and guides action. That sounds as if we have placed little intelligent agents inside the mind. But if edge detection is then explained by simple feature detectors, and those by neural circuitry, and those by biophysical mechanisms, the apparent homunculi are gradually “discharged.” We have not smuggled in a tiny person; we have decomposed an intelligent capacity into organized subcapacities.

This is one reason Dennett valued AI. AI forces philosophers to specify what a capacity would have to do rather than merely name it. The MIT Press description of Brainstorms notes that the 1978 collection was written when cognitive science was emerging and that Dennett wanted philosophers to enter conversation with psychologists, linguists, and computer scientists.

The slogan might be: a mind is made of many mindless or less-minded competences organized in the right way. This is also the cognitive-scientific counterpart of his Darwinism: complex intelligence emerges from simpler routines, not from a central miracle.

⸻

7. Intentionality and content

The traditional problem of intentionality is the problem of “aboutness”: how can a physical state be about Paris, justice, tomorrow, a unicorn, or the square root of two?

Dennett’s answer is neither Cartesian nor classically representationalist. He does not begin with conscious acts of meaning. Nor does he posit intrinsically meaningful inner symbols whose semantic properties are primitive. Instead, content arises through the role a state or system plays in a broader pattern of behavior, interpretation, environmental sensitivity, design history, and rational attribution.

A system’s states count as being about the world when interpreting them that way helps us explain and predict the system as a whole. This is not unconstrained projection. The system’s evolutionary history, learning history, environment, sensory capacities, and behavioral profile all constrain the best interpretation.

Dennett’s content theory is therefore externalist and pragmatic. Content is not sealed inside the head. What a state means depends partly on the system’s relations to the world and on the functional role the state plays in guiding behavior. This is why his theory is hospitable to animals, machines, and distributed systems. It asks not, “Does it have the mysterious inner glow of meaning?” but, “Is there a stable, projectable, interpretation-worthy pattern of world-directed competence?”

This view also explains his gradualism. There is no single moment in evolution when intentionality suddenly appears. There are simpler and richer forms of aboutness: bacterial sensitivity, animal perception, learned expectation, tool use, linguistic representation, self-conscious reflection, scientific theory. Human intentionality is special, but it is not metaphysically discontinuous from more primitive forms of adaptive world-directedness.

⸻

8. Folk psychology: strategy, not inner anatomy

Dennett’s attitude toward folk psychology is subtle. Folk psychology is our everyday practice of explaining action through belief, desire, intention, fear, hope, expectation, and reason. Some philosophers treat folk psychology as a proto-scientific theory whose posits may turn out false, as phlogiston did. Dennett instead treats it primarily as a strategy.

When we explain why someone took an umbrella by saying she believed it might rain and wanted to stay dry, we are not necessarily identifying two inner objects. We are placing her behavior inside an intentional pattern. That pattern can be true, explanatory, and predictive even if no neural item corresponds one-to-one to “the belief that it might rain.”

The Guardian’s obituary notes that Dennett later described himself as a “mild realist” about mental states and treated the traditional psychological perspective as more than mere third-person projection. That captures the mature balance: folk psychology is not a literal map of inner furniture, but neither is it empty mythology.

This makes Dennett’s philosophy of psychology neither eliminativist nor conservative. He preserves belief-desire explanation but revises its ontology. We keep the intentional vocabulary because it tracks real patterns; we give up the assumption that every ordinary mental noun names a private mental object.

⸻

9. Heterophenomenology: the method for studying consciousness

Dennett’s method for consciousness science is heterophenomenology. It is his proposed alternative to both behaviorism and introspective foundationalism.

The method begins by taking subjects’ reports seriously. If a subject says, “I saw a red flash,” “I felt pain,” or “I had a vivid image,” the report is a datum. But the scientist does not treat it as incorrigible access to a private object. Instead, the report is interpreted as evidence about what the subject believes, judges, says, remembers, discriminates, and is disposed to do.

Dennett presents heterophenomenology as continuous with the actual methods of psychophysics, cognitive psychology, clinical neuropsychology, and empirical consciousness research. PhilPapers summarizes his 2007 “Heterophenomenology Reconsidered” as saying that heterophenomenology is not a novelty but the method already used by serious scientific studies of consciousness.

This method is not crude behaviorism because it includes verbal reports, subjective descriptions, and the subject’s own perspective as data. But it is not classical phenomenology because it refuses to treat first-person appearances as self-certifying foundations.

The key move is this: the data are not private qualia; the data are public reports, discriminations, reactions, memories, and dispositions that express how things seem to the subject. Science then explains those seemings.

This is why Dennett often says consciousness must be explained by explaining why people say and judge the things they do about consciousness. Critics think this misses experience itself. Dennett thinks that objection assumes precisely the Cartesian picture that needs to be rejected.

⸻

10. Consciousness: the Multiple Drafts Model

Dennett’s best-known theory of consciousness is the Multiple Drafts Model, developed in Consciousness Explained (1991). The model rejects the idea that there is a single privileged location or moment where contents become conscious. According to the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Dennett’s model treats consciousness in terms of multiple “content fixations” occurring throughout the brain; what makes contents conscious is not their arrival at a Cartesian Theater or special inner format.

The model has several central claims.

First, the brain processes information in parallel, through many partially independent channels. There is no single mental screen on which results are displayed.

Second, contents become influential to different degrees. Some guide behavior, some enter memory, some shape verbal report, some affect future cognition, some quickly disappear.

Third, there is no sharp, intrinsic fact about exactly when a content “entered consciousness” independent of later probes, reports, and behavioral contexts. Different experimental questions may elicit different answers, and there may be no deeper probe-independent fact of the matter.

Fourth, consciousness is not an extra property added to a content after processing is complete. There is no final glow. Consciousness is a matter of the role a content plays in the organism’s ongoing cognitive economy.

Dennett later redescribed this in terms of “fame in the brain.” A content is conscious not because it appears before an inner witness, but because it becomes sufficiently influential or “famous” within the system: available to memory, report, reasoning, attention, and action. Sweet Dreams returns to this model and presents “fame in the brain” as a recasting of the Multiple Drafts view.

The theory is intentionally deflationary. Consciousness is not denied; it is demystified. The mistake is to imagine that conscious experience must involve a final inner presentation. For Dennett, there are only the multiple processes, their interactions, their effects, and the interpretive patterns they support.

⸻

11. The attack on qualia

Dennett’s most controversial doctrine is his rejection of qualia, at least as philosophers have typically conceived them.

Qualia are often introduced as the private, intrinsic, ineffable, directly apprehended “what-it-is-like” properties of experience: the redness of red, the painfulness of pain, the taste of coffee, the subjective feel of warmth. Dennett’s “Quining Qualia” directly attacks this notion. PhilPapers summarizes the familiar examples: the visual quality of milk at sunset, the taste of milk, the sound of swallowing, and other supposed properties of conscious experience.

Dennett’s objection is not that people never see colors, feel pain, taste coffee, or undergo perceptual episodes. His objection is that philosophers have added an incoherent metaphysical posit: intrinsic, private, ineffable, incorrigibly known phenomenal properties that float free of functional, behavioral, cognitive, relational, and environmental facts.

His strategy is to “quine” qualia: deny that the term successfully refers to anything theoretically useful or coherent. The ordinary phenomena remain. The philosophical residue is rejected.

This matters because qualia are often used to generate the “hard problem” of consciousness. If experience contains intrinsic phenomenal properties beyond all functional and cognitive facts, then no functional explanation could explain consciousness. Dennett’s response is to reject the premise. Once all the functional, cognitive, discriminative, affective, reactive, mnemonic, linguistic, and behavioral facts are explained, nothing further remains except a bad philosophical picture.

In contemporary terms, Dennett is often classified as an illusionist about phenomenal consciousness. Illusionism does not mean that nothing happens when we see red or feel pain. It means introspection misrepresents what happens, leading us to believe in special private phenomenal properties. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s entry on qualia discusses illusionism as the view, associated with Dennett and others, that the relevant notion of qualia is illusory rather than an extra ingredient in reality.

Dennett himself later defended illusionism as the conservative default: before positing magical phenomenal properties, we should exhaust mundane explanations of why consciousness seems the way it does.

⸻

12. “Explaining away” versus explaining

A common criticism is that Dennett does not explain consciousness but explains it away. This criticism is not accidental; it touches the heart of his method.

Dennett’s reply is that many successful explanations do partly “explain away” the manifest image. To explain life is to explain away élan vital. To explain combustion is to explain away phlogiston. To explain disease is often to explain away demons or miasmas. In each case, the phenomenon remains, but its naive interpretation is eliminated.

Similarly, Dennett wants to preserve the real phenomena: perception, pain behavior, discrimination, surprise, attention, memory, report, emotional salience, practical control, self-interpretation, and the felt importance of experience. What he rejects is the Cartesian-qualitative gloss: the idea that all this requires an inner theater filled with private mental paint.

This is why Dennett’s title Consciousness Explained was provocative. Google Books describes the work as a full-scale exploration of human consciousness that rejects the traditional commonsense theory and draws on neuroscience, psychology, and AI.

Whether this is explanation or evasion depends on whether one thinks phenomenal consciousness is an explanandum over and above all functional, cognitive, and behavioral facts. Dennett says no. Chalmers, Nagel, Searle, Block, and many phenomenologists say yes, in different ways.

⸻

13. The self as a center of narrative gravity

Dennett’s view of the self is one of the most elegant extensions of his general ontology of real patterns.

In “The Self as a Center of Narrative Gravity,” he compares the self to a center of gravity. A center of gravity is not an extra physical object, yet it is a legitimate abstraction with explanatory value. Likewise, the self is not a soul, Cartesian ego, or neural nucleus. It is an abstract center around which a person’s narratives, dispositions, memories, social roles, plans, and interpretations are organized.

The self is “fictional” in a technical sense, not in the sense of being useless or unreal. It is constituted by interpretive practices. We tell stories about ourselves; others tell stories about us; legal, moral, and social systems stabilize those stories; memory and language allow the story to extend through time. The result is a self.

This view explains several things at once.

It explains why introspection does not reveal a simple inner ego. When we look inward, we find perceptions, memories, feelings, thoughts, impulses, and narratives, but no additional self-substance.

It explains why personal identity can be indeterminate in unusual cases: split-brain phenomena, dissociation, dementia, radical psychological change, and hypothetical duplication cases. If the story ceases to cohere around one center, there may be no single deep fact that settles identity.

It explains why selfhood is socially and linguistically mediated. Human selves are not merely biological control systems; they are narrative artifacts shaped by public language, norms, autobiography, and interpretation.

And it unifies Dennett’s ontology: beliefs, selves, meanings, and centers of gravity are all abstracta that can be real if they organize predictive, explanatory patterns.

⸻

14. Language, culture, and the “Joycean machine”

Dennett gives language an unusually deep role in human consciousness. Nonhuman animals may have perception, learning, memory, and intentionality in important degrees, but human consciousness is transformed by public language, narrative, and culture.

In Consciousness Explained, he describes human consciousness as involving something like a “Joycean machine”: a culturally installed, language-based, serializing layer running on the brain’s massively parallel architecture. This metaphor expresses a central thesis: the apparent unity and narrativity of consciousness are not primitive. They are constructed.

Language allows contents to be rehearsed, reported, stabilized, criticized, remembered, and shared. It turns fleeting cognitive events into objects of reflection. It allows the organism to take the intentional stance toward itself. It enables autobiographical selfhood, explicit planning, moral responsibility, and theoretical thought.

This is where Dennett’s philosophy of mind becomes a philosophy of enculturation. The human mind is not merely the human brain. It is the brain as reorganized by language, tools, symbols, norms, institutions, and other minds.

This is also where his controversial interest in memes enters. He treats memes as culturally transmitted patterns that can reorganize cognition. The Guardian notes that Dennett used memes to explain how cultural practices, including language, can be transmitted between brains and transform brains into minds.

Even if one rejects memetics as a rigorous science, the broader point remains central: for Dennett, mature human consciousness is not simply given by biology. It is biology plus cultural software.

⸻

15. Evolutionary naturalism: from bacteria to Bach

Dennett’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from Darwin. Natural selection shows how design-like organization can arise without a designer. This matters for mind because intentionality, intelligence, and consciousness all look like they require top-down purpose. Dennett’s Darwinian move is to show how apparent purpose can arise bottom-up.

Kinds of Minds presents minds from an evolutionary perspective, beginning with simple world-responsive systems and moving toward animals, humans, language, and reflective thought. Google Books summarizes the book as combining philosophy, AI, and neurobiology, and as treating questions about animals, robots, other minds, and human distinctiveness from an evolutionary perspective.

From Bacteria to Bach and Back carries this project further. A review in PhilSci-Archive describes it as an ambitious attempt to integrate a theory of the evolution of the human mind with a theory of cultural evolution, including a liberalized defense of memes and cultural natural selection.

The key evolutionary notions are:

Competence can precede comprehension. A spider can spin a web without understanding engineering. A bacterium can respond adaptively without representing reasons. An evolved system can do the right thing without knowing why.

Design can precede designers. Evolution produces organs and behaviors that are for something, even though no conscious designer intended them.

Reasons can be “free-floating.” There may be a reason why a trait works even if no creature represents that reason.

Conscious comprehension is late. Human explicit understanding is built from vast layers of uncomprehending competence.

This gives Dennett a powerful answer to a classic worry: “How can meaning come from meaningless matter?” His answer is: gradually, through selection, learning, use, interpretation, and cultural amplification. Meaning is not injected into matter by consciousness; consciousness is a late product of systems already organized in content-like ways.

⸻

16. Agency and free will

Although the user’s question is primarily about mind and psychology, Dennett’s theory of agency belongs to the same system. In Elbow Room and Freedom Evolves, he defends a compatibilist account of free will. The relevant kind of freedom is not exemption from causality. It is the evolved capacity to anticipate, deliberate, avoid, learn, respond to reasons, regulate behavior, and participate in practices of responsibility. MIT Press describes Elbow Room as a major work arguing for compatibilism.

Free will, for Dennett, is another case where a confused metaphysical demand obscures a real phenomenon. Libertarians demand ultimate origination outside deterministic or probabilistic causality. Dennett thinks that is not a freedom worth wanting. The real freedom worth wanting is the difference between a creature that can foresee, evaluate, inhibit, coordinate, negotiate, and act under norms, and a creature that cannot.

This fits perfectly with his broader philosophy. Agency is a real pattern at the personal level. Responsibility is not found by inspecting neurons for guilt. It is found in the social, rational, anticipatory capacities of whole persons.

The Guardian summarizes Dennett’s Darwinian approach to free will as the idea that the physics did not change, but evolved “can do” capacities did.

⸻

17. Dennett’s philosophy of psychology

Dennett’s contribution to psychology is not a specific empirical model of memory, perception, or emotion. It is a philosophy of psychological explanation.

For Dennett, psychology must avoid three errors.

The first error is introspectionism: treating the subject’s inner reports as direct access to private facts. Dennett thinks introspection is theory-laden, reconstructive, partial, and often confabulatory.

The second error is reductionist neurocentrism: assuming that genuine psychological explanation must always be replaced by neural explanation. Dennett insists that personal-level and intentional-level patterns are often indispensable.

The third error is Cartesian cognitive science: replacing the immaterial soul with an internal executive, central processor, or theater. A scientific theory of mind must not reintroduce a little person inside the head.

Psychology, properly understood, should connect intentional interpretation, behavioral data, reports, cognitive architecture, neural mechanisms, developmental history, and evolutionary design. That is why Dennett’s work constantly crosses boundaries among philosophy, AI, neuroscience, ethology, and cognitive psychology. Britannica notes that he made autodidactic forays into AI, neuroscience, and cognitive psychology, and that he believed productive debate about the mind required serious engagement with science.

His philosophy of psychology can be stated as follows: mental states are real at the level at which they support disciplined interpretation, prediction, and explanation of whole agents; psychological science must then explain how subpersonal mechanisms make those patterns possible.

⸻

18. Dennett’s relation to major alternatives

Dennett versus Descartes: Descartes treats consciousness as the secure foundation and the self as a thinking substance. Dennett treats consciousness and selfhood as constructed achievements.

Dennett versus Ryle: Dennett inherits Ryle’s anti-Cartesianism but adds cognitive science, AI, computation, and neuroscience. Ryle attacked the ghost in the machine; Dennett explains how the machine can generate the appearance of a ghost.

Dennett versus behaviorism: Dennett rejects private mental objects, but he is not a behaviorist. He allows internal mechanisms, representations in a loose subpersonal sense, computational architectures, and rich cognitive science.

Dennett versus identity theory: Dennett is a physicalist, but he rejects simple type-identities between ordinary mental states and brain states. The belief that coffee is in the kitchen is not likely to be identical to a single neural state type.

Dennett versus Fodor: Fodor-style computationalism treats propositional attitudes as relations to internal representations, often in a language of thought. Dennett treats belief attribution as stance-relative pattern recognition, not as a commitment to inner sentence-like objects.

Dennett versus Churchland: Churchland-style eliminativism suggests folk psychology may be radically false. Dennett thinks folk psychology captures real patterns, though not in the naive inner-object way.

Dennett versus Searle: Searle argues that syntax is not sufficient for semantics and emphasizes biological subjectivity. Dennett replies, in effect, that understanding is a distributed, functional, interpretive achievement, not an intrinsic biological glow.

Dennett versus Nagel: Nagel emphasizes the irreducible “what it is like” of experience. Dennett argues that this invites a misleading picture of private facts beyond third-person science.

Dennett versus Chalmers: Chalmers formulates the hard problem as the problem of why physical processes give rise to experience. Dennett argues that the hard problem is generated by bad intuitions about qualia and consciousness. Sweet Dreams explicitly targets the “Zombic Hunch” and Chalmers-style hard-problem reasoning.

Dennett versus Block: Block distinguishes access consciousness from phenomenal consciousness. Dennett accepts access-like phenomena but denies that there is an additional phenomenal residue.

Dennett versus phenomenology: Phenomenologists often accuse Dennett of bypassing lived experience. Dennett replies that lived-experience reports must be included, but only within heterophenomenological science.

⸻

19. The systematic philosophy of mind extracted from Dennett

A comprehensive Dennettian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as the following set of theses.

1. Ontological physicalism. Nothing mental exists outside the physical world. There are no souls, immaterial egos, irreducible phenomenal properties, or nonphysical semantic powers.

2. Anti-Cartesian architecture. The mind is not organized around an inner theater, central witness, or privileged point of presentation. Consciousness is distributed, temporally smeared, and functionally constructed.

3. Stance-relative realism. Some phenomena are real only at certain levels of abstraction. Beliefs, selves, meanings, and conscious contents may be real patterns without being concrete inner objects.

4. Intentional systems theory. A system has beliefs and desires insofar as its behavior is reliably and fruitfully predictable from the intentional stance. The richer the pattern, the more robust the intentional attribution.

5. Functionalism without inner-object realism. Mental states are not individuated by their intrinsic stuff but by their role in the organization of agents. But this does not require a simple inventory of inner representations corresponding to folk-psychological terms.

6. Personal/subpersonal pluralism. Psychological explanation must preserve the difference between whole-agent explanations and mechanistic explanations of components. Persons believe and decide; neural subsystems process, detect, regulate, and transmit.

7. Homuncular decomposition. Intelligent capacities are explained by decomposing them into organized subcapacities, each less intelligent than the whole, until the explanation bottoms out in nonintentional mechanisms.

8. Heterophenomenological method. First-person reports are indispensable data, but they are not incorrigible revelations. The science of consciousness studies what subjects say, believe, discriminate, remember, and do, then explains why.

9. Multiple Drafts consciousness. Consciousness is not a final presentation but the career of contents through a distributed cognitive system. A content’s consciousness consists in its functional influence, availability, and role, not in a private glow.

10. Qualia eliminativism or illusionism. The ordinary phenomena of experience are real; philosophical qualia are not. There are pains, colors, tastes, and experiences, but not intrinsic, ineffable, private phenomenal atoms beyond functional explanation.

11. Narrative selfhood. The self is a center of narrative gravity: a real abstract pattern constituted by interpretation, memory, language, social practice, and behavioral coherence.

12. Darwinian gradualism. Mind emerges gradually from simpler forms of adaptive competence. There is no sharp metaphysical line between nonmind and mind, only increasing complexity of intentional organization.

13. Cultural augmentation. Human minds are biologically evolved systems transformed by language, tools, symbols, norms, and cultural transmission. Mature human consciousness is partly a cultural artifact.

14. Compatibilist agency. Free will and responsibility are evolved personal-level capacities for anticipation, self-control, reason-responsiveness, and social accountability, not exemptions from causality.

Together these theses form a unified philosophy: minds are real patterns in evolved, embodied, culturally scaffolded control systems, interpretable from the intentional stance and explainable through subpersonal mechanisms without any Cartesian remainder.

⸻

20. Strengths of Dennett’s position

Dennett’s first strength is methodological discipline. He refuses to let philosophy posit mysteries where explanatory work is required. If consciousness seems magical, the task is to explain how the magic trick works.

His second strength is level pluralism. He understands that not all real patterns appear at the same explanatory level. This lets him avoid both crude reductionism and supernaturalism.

His third strength is integration with science. Dennett did not treat philosophy of mind as isolated from empirical research. He made philosophy answerable to psychology, AI, neuroscience, ethology, and evolutionary theory.

His fourth strength is anti-reification. He repeatedly exposes the temptation to turn useful nouns into inner objects: belief, image, quale, self, will, meaning. His work is a sustained therapy against misleading mental ontologies.

His fifth strength is evolutionary continuity. Instead of treating human consciousness as an inexplicable rupture in nature, he embeds it in a graded story of competence, design, learning, culture, and language.

His sixth strength is his treatment of abstraction. His “real patterns” approach gives philosophers a way to be realist about beliefs, selves, and meanings without treating them as hidden concrete things.

⸻

21. Main objections and vulnerabilities

The deepest objection is that Dennett changes the subject. Critics argue that he explains report, discrimination, access, and behavior but not subjective experience itself. For these critics, the very datum is that there is something it is like to undergo experience, and Dennett’s theory avoids rather than explains that datum.

The second objection is that illusionism is unstable. If consciousness is an illusion, to whom does the illusion appear? Dennett’s answer is that no inner audience is needed: the system is disposed to judge, report, and react as if there were private qualia. Critics find this unsatisfying because it seems to presuppose the very seeming it explains.

The third objection is that heterophenomenology may flatten first-person life. Treating subjective reports as texts to be interpreted may miss the immediacy of lived experience.

The fourth objection is that the intentional stance may be too pragmatic to ground determinate content. If beliefs are patterns relative to interpretive strategies, what fixes exactly which belief someone has? Dennett’s answer is often: nothing more precise than the best interpretive pattern. Critics see this as indeterminacy; Dennett sees it as honesty.

The fifth objection is that the rationality assumption idealizes too much. Human beings are biased, fragmented, inconsistent, self-deceived, and irrational. Dennett can reply that idealization is normal in science, but the worry remains that intentional interpretation may over-rationalize agents.

The sixth objection is that memetics is underdeveloped. Dennett’s cultural-evolutionary story is suggestive, but many critics doubt whether memes provide a rigorous empirical framework.

The seventh objection is that Dennett’s anti-Cartesianism may be too aggressive. Some philosophers agree that there is no inner theater but still think phenomenal consciousness is real and irreducible. Dennett tends to treat this as a relapse into Cartesianism, which opponents regard as unfair.

⸻

22. Final assessment

Dennett’s philosophy of mind is one of the most systematic attempts to naturalize mind without either eliminating psychology or surrendering to dualism. Its governing thought is that the mind is not a thing added to the body. It is a pattern of organization, interpretation, control, history, and cultural amplification.

The best single-sentence reconstruction is:

A mind is an evolved, embodied, environmentally embedded, culturally scaffolded intentional system whose beliefs, self, agency, and consciousness are real higher-level patterns, explainable without private qualia, inner theaters, or Cartesian subjects.

The importance of Dennett’s work lies not merely in particular theses such as the intentional stance or Multiple Drafts. It lies in the architecture: he gives philosophy of mind a way to speak of meaning, consciousness, persons, selves, and responsibility while refusing every appeal to mental magic. That is why his work remains unavoidable even for those who think he is wrong. His opponents often define themselves by insisting that something has been left out. Dennett’s challenge is that, once the mechanisms, patterns, reports, dispositions, narratives, and practices are fully explained, we must say exactly what that “something” is supposed to be.

PreviousThomas Nagel NextJohn Searle

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
